title: Beyond 2D: The Time Dimension
concept: To analyze video, neural networks must move beyond 2D spatial features and learn features across the temporal (time) dimension.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main issue with frame-by-frame 2D video processing?

options.text: It has no memory of previous frames, ignoring motion
It can only process grayscale video
It makes the video files larger on disk

exercises.explain: Correct! 2D convolutions on individual frames lack temporal context, meaning they cannot capture motion.

cards.title: Stepping into 3D
cards.body: When we analyze static images, we use flat 2D filters that look at height and width. But a video is a stack of frames over time — a 3D volume! To find patterns that move, our neural networks need to step into the third dimension: time.

cards.title: Spatiotemporal Learning
cards.body: Spatiotemporal learning is the process of extracting features that span both space (height/width) and time (frames). Instead of treating time separately, spatiotemporal models learn them simultaneously.

cards.title: Biting a layered sandwich
cards.body: If you eat each layer of a club sandwich separately, you never experience the combined flavor. A spatiotemporal model is like taking a big bite straight down through all the layers (bread, cheese, tomato) at once!

cards.title: Detecting a jump
cards.body: A 2D model sees a person crouched, then mid-air, then standing. A spatiotemporal model processes these frames together to detect the continuous motion of jumping, learning the speed and height of the action.

cards.title: Not just recurrent layers
cards.body: You might think we should just use a 2D CNN for images and an RNN (like LSTM) for time. While possible, that separates space and time! Convolving directly in 3D allows the network to learn low-level motion features much earlier.

cards.title: 2D vs. 3D Features
cards.body: Comparing spatial-only and spatiotemporal feature learning:

left.text:
Extracts edges, shapes, colors
Analyzes height and width
Ignores frame sequences

right.text:
Extracts motion, speed, trajectories
Analyzes height, width, and time
Finds patterns across sequences

exercises.prompt: What does 'spatiotemporal' refer to in video processing?

options.text: A combination of spatial (space) and temporal (time) features
A method to increase video resolution
A filter that converts color video to black and white
The speed at which a video is downloaded

exercises.explain: Correct! Spatiotemporal refers to both spatial (space/pixels) and temporal (time/frames) dimensions.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Spatiotemporal learning processes multiple video frames together rather than one by one.
exercises.explain: True. To extract temporal features, the network must analyze sequence patterns across multiple frames simultaneously.

exercises.sentence: While 2D models focus purely on spatial shapes, spatiotemporal models learn how those shapes change over ___.
exercises.explain: Spatiotemporal learning maps changes in spatial features over time to understand actions.

exercises.prompt: Which of these tasks is a classic example of spatiotemporal learning?

options.text: Recognizing a gesture like a thumbs-up or hand wave
Identifying a static flower species in a photo
Segmenting the background of a portrait shot

exercises.explain: A hand wave is a gesture that requires tracking motion over time, making it a spatiotemporal task.

exercises.prompt: Why is a spatiotemporal model better at recognizing a person running than a model that classifies single frames and averages the scores?

options.text: Because running is defined by the motion sequence (legs swinging, feet pushing), which averaging single frames ignores.
Because spatiotemporal models always output higher resolution predictions.
Because a person running has no spatial features in individual frames.
Because averaging scores causes the GPU to run out of memory.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Running is a temporal pattern of coordination. Averaging single frame predictions tells you there is a person, but loses the temporal rhythm of the stride. Next: how do we actually perform convolutions on these 3D video volumes?
